summary: I was born in Carthage, Texas, not in a hospital but in a doctor's clinic run by Dr. Hooker. My family lived six miles outside of Carthage at a natural gas company compressor station before moving back into town after my father returned from World War II. I attended school in Carthage for twelve years, riding the school bus every day. Carthage was my hometown and the center of much of my early life and family history.

My mother, Tula Hightower, was born in St. Augustine, Texas, and moved to Carthage as a child. She was a sharecropper's daughter during the Great Depression and later became deeply involved in the church and community. Despite not being raised in the church, she became a devoted Christian and active in the United Methodist Women. She also wrote a weekly devotional column for the Panola Watchmen for twenty-five years and was a fitness enthusiast well into her sixties and seventies.

My mother was born in St. Augustine, Texas, which is about sixty miles south of Carthage. Her family moved from there to Carthage when she was a child, so although I was born in Carthage, my mother's roots trace back to St. Augustine. It was a farming community where her family worked as sharecroppers during the Great Depression.

My mother's family were sharecroppers during the Great Depression, working very hard but only getting half of what they produced. My father also grew up during that time and had to leave school to help support his family. The hardships of the Great Depression shaped both my parents' values and their determination to see their children go to college.

I was just a baby when the Japanese bombed Pearl Harbor in December 1941, which marked the beginning of the United States' involvement in World War II. My father was drafted two years later in 1943 and sent off to fight. That event changed our family life dramatically, as we had to move out of our company house and live with my grandparents during the war.
cues: start of World War II for US

summary: World War II had a big impact on my family. My father was drafted in 1943 and served in the 86th Infantry Division in Germany, part of Patton's Third Army during the Battle of Bastogne. After the war in Europe ended, his unit was sent to the Philippines to prepare for the invasion of Japan. When he returned, our family moved back to the company house near Carthage, and life slowly returned to normal.

My father fought in Germany during World War II with the 86th Infantry Division. He was part of Patton’s Third Army that rushed to Bastogne in the bitter winter of 1944. He saw a lot of action there in the final year of the war in Europe before being sent to the Philippines afterward.

My mother became very involved in the church after the war, especially with the United Methodist Women, which was known then as the Women's Society of Christian Service. She attended meetings regularly and was active in church life, including Sunday services and prayer meetings. Her faith and church involvement deeply influenced our family.

My mother dreamed that all her children would attend Southern Methodist University, or SMU, because it was big and Methodist and she believed that made it good. I, my sister Jacqueline, and my brother Warren all graduated from SMU, and now both of my sons are SMU graduates as well. SMU has been a central part of our family's educational journey.

My sister Jacqueline married a Texas A&M engineer named Manual Rodriguez, and their two sons are also Aggie graduates. So Texas A&M became part of our extended family's educational story, complementing the SMU tradition in our immediate family.

My brother Warren was just the right age to be drafted into the Vietnam War. He served with distinction and came back to become a Dallas police officer before eventually returning to school at SMU for advanced banking studies. His service in Vietnam was a significant chapter in his life and ours.

After returning from Vietnam, my brother Warren worked as a Dallas police officer for eight years. He found it difficult to make a living as a policeman, so he went back to school at SMU and later became a bank president in our hometown. His time with the Dallas Police Department was an important part of his career path.

My mother wrote a weekly devotional column called 'First Things First' for the Panola Watchman, a small weekly paper in Carthage. She started after someone suggested publishing her devotional for Church Women United, and it became very popular, running for twenty-five years. That column was a significant way she contributed to our community.

I once read in the National Education Association Journal that a child who lives with praise will develop a sense of destiny. This idea resonated with me because my mother always told me and my siblings that we were special and had a purpose. That encouragement helped shape my belief that we were sent to this time and place to do something significant.

My father’s full name was Marvin Mouzon Biggs Sr., named after two Methodist bishops, Enoch Marvin and Edwin Dubose Mouzon. He was the only son in his family and worked for a natural gas company for forty-three years, eventually becoming superintendent of East Texas operations. He never finished high school but was a respected community member who served on the hospital board and deeply valued education for his children.

Enoch Marvin was one of the two Methodist bishops after whom my father was named. My grandparents had heard him preach and honored him by giving his name to my father. This connection to Methodist heritage was important in our family identity.

Edwin Dubose Mouzon was the other Methodist bishop whose name was given to my father as a middle name. He was French American and respected by my grandparents, who chose to honor both bishops in naming my father. This reflected our family's Methodist roots and values.

My father served in the 86th Infantry Division, also known as the Black Hawk Division, during World War II. He saw significant combat in Germany, including the Battle of Bastogne, as part of Patton’s Third Army. After the war in Europe ended, his unit was sent to the Philippines to prepare for the invasion of Japan.

My father was part of General Patton’s Third United States Army during World War II. They rushed to Bastogne in the harsh winter of 1944, a pivotal moment in the Battle of the Bulge. His service with this army was a defining experience of his military career.

The Battle of Bastogne was one of the major battles my father fought in during World War II. It was a cold and difficult winter in 1944, and he was part of the forces that rushed to relieve the siege. This battle was a significant and harrowing part of his wartime experience.

After the war in Europe ended, my father’s unit was sent to the Philippines to prepare for the planned invasion of Japan. This was part of MacArthur’s army buildup in the Pacific theater. Fortunately, the war ended before that invasion took place, and my father returned home to Carthage.

General Douglas MacArthur was the commander of the army my father joined in the Philippines after fighting in Europe. They were preparing for the invasion of Japan under MacArthur’s leadership. This connection placed my father in a significant part of the Pacific theater at the end of World War II.

In seventh grade, my mother gave me a book by Norman Vincent Peale called The Power of Positive Thinking. That book taught me to focus on my strengths and helped me overcome my shyness. It was one of the early influences that shaped my attitude and confidence growing up.

The Power of Positive Thinking by Norman Vincent Peale was a book my mother encouraged me to read when I was in seventh grade. It taught me to concentrate on what I do best and helped me develop a positive mindset during my shy childhood. This book was an important influence on my personal development.

Along with Peale’s book, I also read Dale Carnegie’s How to Win Friends and Influence People. These books together helped me learn social skills and build confidence. They were part of my early efforts to improve myself and succeed academically and socially.

entity: How to Win Friends and Influence People
summary: How to Win Friends and Influence People by Dale Carnegie was another book my mother encouraged me to read. It taught me valuable lessons about interacting with others and gaining influence. Reading it helped me overcome shyness and become a better student and person.
